scenes.phrase: You've paid Spotify more money every single year since 2023

Where money goes. Why artists quit. App worse. Podcasts. Fake numbers. Weapons.
This is not a hit piece. This is an audit. Where did your $11.99 actually go?
2021. Spotify Premium cost $9.99. It had cost $9.99 since 2014. Seven years, zero increases.
You pay $10, get every song ever recorded. Best deal in history of consumer media. Insane.
June 2023. First hike. $10.99. Spotify blamed market conditions — the real reason was simpler.
Wall Street demanded growth, so they started squeezing the ones they already had.
April 2024. Second hike. $11.99. They called it expansion of features.
Audiobooks you didn't ask for, added anyway. That's not a feature. That's a hostage.
July 2025. Third hike. Individual $12.99, Family $19.99, Duo $17.99 — a psychological trick.
2026. Fourth hike. $14.99. You are now paying 50% more than 3 years ago. Same app.

Which there usually isn't. That $10 billion headline? It's the top-line number, before every middleman in the chain takes a bite. The amount that actually hits an artist's bank account is

a fraction of a fraction of a fraction. Now let's talk per stream. The widely cited industry estimate for Spotify's per-stream payout is between 3/10 of a cent and 5/10 of a cent. 3 tenths of a cent to one half of a cent per
stream. To make minimum wage in the United States, $15 an hour, which is itself a low bar, an artist would need roughly 4,000 streams per hour. To make the average American salary

scenes.phrase: the average song on Spotify gets about 30 streams per month. Not 30,000. 30. If you're a normal independent artist with a small but loyal audience,
say 2,000 monthly listeners, which is actually pretty good for an indie, you are generating something like 60,000 streams a month. At 3/10 of a cent per stream, that's $180
a month. Before distributor fees. Before label cuts. Before taxes. $180 for a month of work that probably took a year to make. And it gets worse. Because Spotify doesn't pay a flat rate per stream.
The payout is determined by something called the revenue share model. Here's how it actually works. Every month, Spotify pools all of its subscription revenue from every country.

They take their cut, roughly 30%, off the top. Then they divide what's left by the total number of streams across the entire platform, weighted by where those streams came from. A stream from a US subscriber is worth

scenes.phrase: And they are being underpaid. By every possible measure. In 2014, the per-stream payout was around 7 tenths of a cent.
Today, it's 3 tenths of a cent. In a decade, the value of a single stream has dropped by more than half. And in that same decade, Spotify's revenue has more than tripled. From $5
billion in 2019 to over $15 billion in 2024. The pie got 3 times bigger. The slice that goes to artists got twice as small. That's not a market correction.
That's a structural feature. Spotify has engineered their payout system so that as the company grows, artists get less. On purpose.
You'd think a company that just announced record profits would share the love. Right? Wrong. They announced a new rule instead. And it might be the worst one yet. Every dollar you pay Spotify gets

bandwidth, app development, marketing, podcasts, which we'll get to, and increasingly, AI features that nobody asked for. The other 70 cents goes to rights holders.
Labels, distributors, publishers, and eventually, maybe, artists. But that 70 cents doesn't get split equally. It gets split based on market share. Which means Universal

Music Group, the largest label in the world, gets a much bigger chunk than an independent artist on DistroKid. Even if the independent artist generates the same number of streams as a Universal artist, the Universal artist gets paid more per

scenes.phrase: about to. In November 2023, Spotify quietly announced a change to their royalty system. Quietly is the operative word.

It wasn't a press release. It wasn't an email to artists. It was a blog post on a Spotify for Artists subdomain, written in the driest corporate language possible, dropped on a Friday afternoon. Which,

as anyone in PR knows, is the day you announce things you want people to ignore. Here's the rule: Starting in early 2024, any track that doesn't reach 1,000 streams in a 12-month period earns zero royalties. Zero.

Not less. Spotify framed this as a fight against fraud. They said there were tens of millions of tracks on the platform generating tiny amounts of money— fractions of a cent— and that those tracks were clogging up the system. By zeroing them
out, they said, they could redirect that money to working artists. Sounds reasonable, right? Wrong.

Here's what they didn't tell you. Roughly 2/3 of the tracks on Spotify, somewhere around 65 to 70 million songs, get fewer than 1,000 streams per year. That's not a fringe group of noise tracks. That's the majority of the catalog.

scenes.phrase: Streaming doesn't pay, so every other revenue stream has to compensate. The concert ticket that used to cost $40 now costs $120 because the artist can't make rent from Spotify plays. You think Ticketmaster is the
bad guy. And they are. But Ticketmaster is a symptom. Spotify is the disease. When the world's largest music platform pays artists 3/10 of a cent per stream,
every other part of the music industry has to extract more from you to make up the difference. The middle class of music is dying in front of us. Quietly. Without protest.
Because the artist who would protest can't afford to take a year off to organize. They're too busy trying to pay rent. And Spotify's response?

cmds.text: Because the artist who would protest can't afford …

scenes.phrase: They raised the price of Premium again. And introduced a new feature called, I'm not making this up, AI DJ. But before we get to the AI slop, let's talk about the fraud economy Spotify's
own rules created. Because that's the next domino. In January 2024, Spotify removed a song called Heat of the Moment by an artist named Antonio Lulic. Or rather, they removed billions of streams from
him. Specifically, they accused him of using bots to inflate his play counts and stripped him of around 700 million streams. He wasn't alone. In late 2023 and
throughout 2024, Spotify went on a purge. They removed millions of tracks and billions of streams from artists they accused of streaming fraud. They published a loud-and-clear report claiming
they'd identified and stopped hundreds of millions of dollars in fraudulent payouts. Sounds great, right?
Spotify fighting fraud. Protecting artists wrong. Here's the part they don't put in the press release: Spotify created the fraud problem. The fraud exists because Spotify's payout system

rewards streams above all else. If streams are money, then fake streams are fake money. And fake money is, in any system with a leak, going to be manufactured. The bot farm industry is now worth an estimated hundreds of millions of dollars per
year. There are services, legal services, operating openly, advertising on Google, that will sell you 10,000 Spotify streams for around $10. They use networks of compromised
devices, fake accounts, and residential IP proxies to generate streams that look real to Spotify's detection systems. An artist who's 800 streams short of the demonetization
threshold can pay $15 and cross the line. A label trying to push a new single up the charts can buy half a million streams for a few thousand dollars and trigger algorithmic momentum
that generates real streams. This isn't a fringe problem. Spotify themselves estimated that somewhere between 2 and 4% of all streams on the platform are fake. That's billions of streams per month.
And the actual number is almost certainly higher. Because Spotify's detection is— charitably— mediocre.

cmds.text: And the actual number is almos…

scenes.phrase: Independent analyses have put the fraud rate closer to 10%. Here's what that means in dollar terms. If Spotify pays out around $9 billion a year to rights holders, and even
5% of streams are fraudulent, that's roughly $450 million per year. Going to fake artists, fake labels,
and the bot farms that serve them. $450 million that should've gone to real musicians making real music.

And here's the kicker: that money comes out of the same revenue share pool as everyone else's. Every fake stream is a fraction of a cent stolen from a real artist. When a bot farm generates a million streams for a fake artist, that million
streams' worth of money is deducted from the pool that would have gone to legitimate artists. Spotify knows this.
They've known it for years. They periodically announce crackdowns, like the 2024 purge, to make it look like they're doing something. But the structural problem is theirs to
solve, and they haven't solved it because solving it would require either dramatically improving their detection technology, which costs money,

scenes.phrase: or fundamentally changing the payout model away from pure per-stream revenue share, which would upset the major labels who benefit from the current system. So instead, they publish an annual report,
purge a few scapegoats, and move on. There's another layer to this. Some of the streaming fraud isn't even fraud. It's just artists asking their fans to stream their music. Spotify's terms
of service prohibit artificial streaming, But the line between artificial and organic is blurry. If an artist tells their fans to put their album on repeat while they sleep, is that artificial?

cmds.text: of service prohibit artificial streaming, But the …

scenes.phrase: If a fan runs a playlist on shuffle for 16 hours a day to support their favorite band, is that artificial? Spotify says yes, sometimes. They've flagged legitimate fan streaming as fraud
and pulled royalties from artists who did nothing wrong. So you have real fraud going unpunished at scale, Real artists being punished for fake fraud, and a payout system that incentivizes the whole
mess. Spotify's response is to raise prices and add AI features. We're gonna talk about those AI features next.
Because they are something else. Every single day, Spotify adds over 100,000 new tracks to its catalog.

names. They create their own playlists, or pay for placement on existing ones, and slot their AI tracks into the rotation. Then they sit back and collect fractions of a cent per stream.
Multiplied by thousands of tracks, multiplied by millions of playlist listeners. At scale, this is real money. A single operator running 50 fake artist accounts, each generating
100,000 streams per month, is making something like $7,500 a month. Per operator.
There are now thousands of these operators. The economics are too good to ignore. And it gets worse. Spotify itself has been caught doing this. In 2024, multiple investigations

Just a name, a generic profile picture, and dozens of tracks pumping into playlists with millions of followers. Why would Spotify do this? Because they pay themselves for those streams.

scenes.phrase: The tracks are uploaded through distributors that Spotify either owns or has financial relationships with. When Spotify's editorial team places a ghost artist track in a flagship playlist,
the payout for that stream goes back to Spotify's bottom line. They're laundering their own royalty payments. Spotify denied this, of course. They said the artists were real, just private.

They said the tracks were licensed. They refused to name the artists. They refused to provide evidence. They just denied, and then quietly removed some of the most obvious offenders from their playlists,

The editorial playlists that used to be a path to discovery for real artists are increasingly populated by ghost tracks. And here's the most insidious part. The flood of new tracks,

scenes.phrase: which produced 12 episodes and then collapsed in mutual recrimination. They signed the Obamas. They signed Kim Kardashian.
They bought Parcast. They bought Megaphone. They built a podcast empire. The strategy made sense on paper. Spotify wanted to be the default audio platform. Not just music— audio.
And podcasts had something music didn't: music royalties ate most of the revenue. Podcasts were much cheaper. If Spotify owned the podcast, they didn't have to share the revenue with labels.

They could keep it all. And podcasts had something else: they were engaged. People who listened to podcasts listened for hours per week. Habitual, loyal, captivated. If Spotify could own that engagement, they'd own the listener. So they spent.
And spent. And spent. And it didn't work. Here's what happened. Joe Rogan's exclusive deal drove some listeners to Spotify. But many of his fans, the ones who already used

cmds.text: And spent. And spent. And it didn't work. Here's w…

scenes.phrase: Apple Podcasts or other apps, resented being forced to switch. A meaningful chunk of his audience just stopped listening rather than migrate. And then, when Spotify's exclusivity
period ended, and Rogan went back to being available everywhere, the supposed benefit of the exclusive deal evaporated.

cmds.text: period ended, and Rogan went back to being availab…

scenes.phrase: Spotify paid a quarter of a billion dollars for temporary exclusivity that didn't even last the full contract. Meghan Markle's deal produced a single season of a podcast called Archetypes,

which got middling reviews and then was unceremoniously dumped when the deal fell apart. She and Spotify's executive allegedly didn't get along. A Spotify executive publicly called her and Harry grifters. That's a Spotify executive

cmds.text: which got middling reviews and then was unceremoni…

scenes.phrase: talking about Spotify's own signing. That's how well that billion dollars was spent. The ringer has been successful in some respects. Bill Simmons is a shrewd operator. But it hasn't moved the

cmds.text: talking about Spotify's own signing. That's how we…

scenes.phrase: needle for Spotify's bottom line. Gimlet, which was supposed to be the HBO of podcasts, has been largely gutted.

Several of their flagship shows were canceled, Talented producers left. The promise of high-quality narrative podcasting was sacrificed on the altar of engagement metrics. And then, there's the broader
strategic failure. Spotify tried to make podcasting work the way music works— centralized, algorithmic, exclusive. But podcasting was built on RSS— open,
distributed, platform-agnostic. Listeners had a habit of using whatever app they preferred. Apple Podcasts, Overcast, Pocket Casts. They didn't want to be locked into Spotify.
And creators didn't want to be locked in either. The Joe Rogan exclusivity backfired because creators saw what happened when you signed with Spotify. You got paid, but you lost

cmds.text: And creators didn't want to be locked in either. T…

scenes.phrase: your audience. By 2023, Spotify was quietly retreating from podcasting. They canceled shows. They laid off staff,
including a significant portion of their podcast division. They shut down Parcast. They took shows off exclusive.
They restructured their deals. They wrote down the value of their podcast acquisitions. Meaning, in plain English, they admitted they overpaid. Here's the brutal truth: Spotify
spent over a billion dollars to enter a business that, even at its peak, contributed only a small fraction of their revenue.

cmds.text: contributed only a small fraction o…
spent over a billion dollars to ent…

scenes.phrase: The podcast business still doesn't generate meaningful profit for Spotify. And in the process, they alienated listeners who didn't want to switch apps, alienated creators who didn't want to be locked in, and burned through capital that could have

gone to— oh, I don't know— paying artists more. But the billion dollars is gone. The shows are canceled. The deals have been restructured. And Spotify has moved on to their next pivot: audiobooks. Oh,
audiobooks. Where do I even start with audiobooks? In late 2023, Spotify announced that Premium subscribers would get 15 hours of audiobooks per month included with their subscription.
Great news, right? Audible charges $15 a month for one credit. Spotify is throwing audiobooks into your existing subscription. What's not to like? Here's what's not to like:
15 hours. Per month. That's it. A typical audiobook is 10 to 15 hours. So you get one audiobook per month, maybe 2 short ones if you're lucky. After that, you have to buy more,
and the buying more is where it gets ugly. When you run out of your 15 hours, Spotify prompts you to buy more hours.

cmds.text: and the buying more is where it gets ugly. When yo…

scenes.phrase: They sell 10-hour top-ups for around $13, which is, conveniently, almost exactly what Audible charges for a credit. So Spotify didn't replace Audible. They created a worse Audible, with a worse selection, baked into your music

cmds.text: They sell 10-hour top-ups for around $13, which is…

scenes.phrase: subscription. But here's the real trick. The reason Spotify added audiobooks wasn't to give you a feature. It was to justify the price hike. Remember the April 2024 hike? From $10.99
to $11.99? Spotify explicitly cited the inclusion of audiobooks as the reason. They added a feature most users don't use, and then used that feature as the justification to
raise prices on everyone. Including the people who don't use it. It's a textbook bundling trick. You add a low-value component to a bundle, raise the price of the bundle, and pocket the difference
from the users who never use the new component. Most Spotify subscribers don't listen to audiobooks. Spotify knows this.

But they get to charge everyone an extra dollar or 2 per month because the feature is included. And the audiobook selection itself is rough. Spotify has around 250,000 audiobooks. Audible has over 700,000.
The Spotify catalog is missing huge numbers of major titles. You'll search for a book you want, find it's not there, and be told to either buy it separately at full retail price, or wait for Spotify

cmds.text: You'll search for a book you w…
and be told to either buy it s…

scenes.phrase: to maybe add it eventually. And the authors are getting screwed too. In 2024, the Authors Guild and several prominent authors filed formal complaints against Spotify

cmds.text: And the authors are getting screwed…
the Authors Guild and several promi…

scenes.phrase: over the audiobook bundling. The issue was this: Spotify was paying audiobook royalties based on a formula that essentially treated audiobook listening as a fraction of total listening time,

cmds.text: The issue was this: Spotify wa…

scenes.phrase: similar to how music royalties work. But audiobooks aren't music. A typical audiobook listener spends hours on a single book. The way Spotify's payout math worked,
authors were getting fractions of a cent per hour of listening, which translated to tiny, tiny payouts. For a book that took an author years to write, and that the audiobook narrator spent weeks
recording, the royalty payout under Spotify's system might be a few dollars for thousands of listening hours. Audible pays authors a flat, credit-based royalty that's much more generous.

Spotify's system is by comparison an insult. And there's another wrinkle. Spotify acquired Findaway, an audiobook distribution platform, in 2022. That gave them access to a huge catalog of audiobooks. But it also gave

them leverage to dictate terms to authors and narrators. Findaway narrators, who are paid a flat fee to record books, found that their work was being used to drive Spotify subscriptions without any meaningful additional compensation.

cmds.text: them leverage to dictate terms to a…
found that their work was being use…

scenes.phrase: The narrator who spent 40 hours recording a book might see $10 in additional royalties from Spotify's bundling, while Spotify used their work as a marketing talking point to justify
a price hike. So let's recap. Spotify added audiobooks to justify a price increase. Most subscribers don't use the audiobook feature. The audiobook selection is much

smaller than Audible's. Authors get paid less under Spotify's system than under Audible's. Narrators get paid almost nothing extra. And Spotify gets to keep the additional revenue from the price hike bundling. They call it bundling.
I have a different word for it. But you've probably noticed something. I keep mentioning that Spotify still doesn't have lossless audio.
Let's talk about that broken promise next. Because it's been 5 years. In February 2021, Spotify announced Spotify HiFi. The pitch was simple: lossless, CD-quality audio for premium subscribers.
Higher bitrate, better sound, no compression artifacts. Coming later in 2021. It's 2026.

HiFi still doesn't exist. 5 years. 5 years of a promised feature that has never shipped. Spotify has mentioned HiFi in exactly zero product launches since the original announcement. They've removed references to it from their website.

They've stopped responding to questions about it. Their customer support, when asked, gives vague non-answers like, we're still exploring options. In the same 5 years that Spotify has failed to ship lossless audio, Apple Music shipped it.
For free. Included in the standard subscription. No extra charge. Tidal built their entire brand around it. Amazon Music Unlimited added it at no extra cost.

cmds.text: Tidal built their entire brand arou…
Amazon Music Unlimited added it at …

scenes.phrase: YouTube Music added it. Qobuz has had it for a decade. Every major music streaming service on Earth offers lossless audio. Except Spotify.
Why? Here's the honest answer: Spotify can't afford to ship it. Not because lossless audio is expensive. It isn't, not really. The bandwidth costs are manageable.
But because Spotify's entire infrastructure was built around aggressive audio compression. Their Ogg Vorbis codec at 96 to 320 kilobits per second was designed to minimize bandwidth costs

at scale. Serving billions of streams per day at 320 kilobits per second costs them a certain amount of money. Serving those same streams at 1.4 megabits, CD quality, would cost them roughly 4 times as much in bandwidth. And Spotify operates on thin
margins. Or at least, they claim to. The other reason is strategic. Spotify's not stupid. They know that if they ship lossless audio, they need to either charge more
for it, which would mean another price hike, which would mean another round of bad press, or include it in the existing plan, which would mean absorbing the bandwidth cost. So they've stalled.

cmds.text: which would mean another round of b…
which would mean absorbing the band…

scenes.phrase: They announced HiFi in 2021, to keep up with Apple's announcement that Apple Music was going lossless.

They never had to ship it because their subscribers didn't leave in meaningful numbers. Most people can't tell the difference between 320 kbps and lossless on Bluetooth earbuds anyway. So Spotify quietly killed

cmds.text: They never had to ship it beca…
Most people can't tell the dif…

scenes.phrase: the project and never told anyone. But the promise is still out there. The 2021 announcement is still in archives. People still ask about HiFi in Spotify community forums.

cmds.text: the project and never told anyone. But the promise…

scenes.phrase: Spotify still ignores them. 5 years. This is the kind of small, specific, infuriating failure that defines modern Spotify.
It's not a single catastrophic mistake. It's a thousand small ones. A promised feature that never shipped. A price hike that wasn't justified.
A royalty cut disguised as fraud prevention. A podcast empire that collapsed. An audiobook pivot that nobody asked for.

Each one, in isolation, is a minor story. Together they form a pattern. And the pattern is this: Spotify takes more from you, gives less to artists, and invests the difference in whatever the next big pivot is supposed to be. Podcasts,
audiobooks, AI. Anything except the core product, the music app you pay for, but maybe you don't care about hi-fi.

Maybe you don't care about podcasts. Maybe you just want to know why your Discover Weekly has gotten worse. Let's talk about that. When was the last time Discover Weekly introduced you to an artist you'd never heard of, who you actually loved,
who you then went and followed, and whose music you then sought out on your own? Take your time. For most of you, the answer is, I can't remember. Maybe years ago. Maybe never.

cmds.text: who you then went and followed, and whose music yo…

scenes.phrase: Here's why. When Discover Weekly launched in 2015, It was genuinely magical. The algorithm would analyze your listening history, find patterns, and recommend songs from artists

cmds.text: Here's why. When Discover Weekly launche…

scenes.phrase: you'd never heard but who matched your taste. People would post their Discover Weekly on social media. It was a feature. A real feature.
Something Spotify did that nobody else could do. Fast forward to 2026. Discover Weekly is mostly the same songs you already listen to, Slightly remixed,

cmds.text: Something Spotify did that nobody else c…

scenes.phrase: with one or two unfamiliar tracks buried at the bottom. Release Radar is dominated by major label releases. Your daily mixes have devolved into the same 50 songs on rotation.
Spotify's algorithmic recommendations have, by almost every user's anecdotal experience, gotten worse. Why? Here's the structural reason: Spotify's recommendation algorithm isn't purely

cmds.text: Spotify's algorithmic recommendations have, by alm…

scenes.phrase: designed to find you new music you'd love. It's designed to maximize total listening time. Those sound like the same goal. They're not.

cmds.text: designed to find you new music…
Those sound like the same goal

scenes.phrase: If Spotify wanted to find you new music you'd love, they'd recommend unfamiliar tracks and accept that some of those wouldn't click, leading you to skip, leading to a temporary dip in engagement.

cmds.text: If Spotify wanted to find you new m…

scenes.phrase: They don't do that. Instead, the algorithm plays it safe. It recommends tracks similar to what you already listen to, Because those tracks have a high probability of being played through

cmds.text: They don't do that. Instead, the algorithm plays i…

scenes.phrase: to the end. More plays through to the end means more listening time, more streams, more revenue. The algorithm is optimizing for engagement,
not discovery. And engagement on a per-user basis is maximized by playing it safe. There's also the editorial layer.

cmds.text: And engagement on a per-user basis …

scenes.phrase: Spotify's editorial playlists— Rap Caviar, Today's Top Hits, Hot Country, Rock Classics— are curated by Spotify staff.
But those staff are heavily influenced by major label priorities. Major labels have teams whose entire job is to pitch Spotify editors for playlist placement. They send gifts, they have

relationships, they have leverage. Independent artists don't have any of that. So the playlists that drive discovery are dominated by major label artists. The algorithmic playlists are tuned for engagement rather than discovery. And the long

cmds.text: So the playlists that drive discove…

scenes.phrase: tail of independent music, which Spotify claims to champion, is buried under a pile of major label priorities and safe recommendations. And then there's Discovery Mode, which we covered earlier.
The payola scheme. Major labels can opt their tracks into Discovery Mode, accept a lower royalty rate, and get algorithmic boosts. Independent artists can technically do the same,
but the lower royalty rate, hurts them disproportionately. The result? The algorithm is even more skewed toward major label artists, because they're the ones who can afford to pay for placement.

There's another problem. Spotify's algorithm has a feedback loop problem. If you listen to a track once, even accidentally, even because you skipped to it, the algorithm treats that as a positive signal.

cmds.text: the algorithm treats that as a posi…

scenes.phrase: It then recommends more tracks like that, which you might listen to, which reinforces the signal, which leads to more recommendations of the same kind. Your recommendations can drift

in directions you never intended, based on a single accidental listen. This is why your daily mix sometimes looks like it belongs to a completely different person. The algorithm got confused by a stray listen

cmds.text: in directions you never intended, based on a singl…

scenes.phrase: and decided you must be a fan of, I don't know, German techno. And now you can't get rid of it. And Spotify doesn't give you good tools to fix this. There's no reset my

And Spotify doesn't give you good t…

cmds.text: recommendations button. There's no I don't like th…

scenes.phrase: track. You're stuck with what it thinks of you. The fundamental issue is that Spotify's algorithm isn't working for you. It's working for Spotify. Its job is to maximize your listening

cmds.text: track. You're stuck with what it thinks of you. Th…

scenes.phrase: time, which maximizes their revenue, which they then use to pay artists less per stream while raising your subscription price.
The algorithm is a machine for extracting value from your attention. It just happens to play music while it does it. You'd think, given all of this, that Spotify would at least be cheap.
Right? Wrong. They're the most expensive mainstream music service. And their biggest competitor pays artists twice as much per stream. Let's talk about Apple Music. Apple Music pays
artists roughly double what Spotify pays per stream. Let me just say that again. Apple Music pays artists roughly double what Spotify pays per stream. The widely cited numbers are Spotify,

cmds.text: Let me just say that again
The widely cited numbers are S…

scenes.phrase: 3/10 of a cent to 5/10 of a cent per stream. Apple Music, 7/10 of a cent to 1 cent per stream. Apple pays twice as much. And their subscription price is, in most
markets, the same as Spotify's. Or, after Spotify's 2025 and 2026 hikes, cheaper. Why? Because Apple doesn't need Spotify to make money. Apple makes its money selling iPhones.

cmds.text: markets, the same as Spotify's. Or, after Spotify'…

scenes.phrase: Apple Music exists to make the iPhone ecosystem more attractive. It's a value-add, a loss leader in some quarters.

Apple can afford to pay artists more per stream because Apple doesn't need Apple Music to be massively profitable on its own. Own. The profitability comes from the iPhone you bought to use Apple Music on. Spotify, on the other hand, is just
Spotify. They don't sell phones, they don't sell laptops, they don't have a hardware business subsidizing their software business.

cmds.text: they don't have a hardware business…

scenes.phrase: Every dollar Spotify pays to artists is a dollar that doesn't go to Spotify's profit margin. So they squeeze. And it's not just the per-stream payout. Apple Music has shipped feature after
feature that Spotify hasn't. Lossless audio, Spatial audio with Dolby Atmos. Lyrics that scroll in real time. Live lyrics that you can sing along to. Apple Music Sing, which lets you mute the vocals

and karaoke any song. All included in the base subscription. All free. Spotify has, in the same period, raised prices 4 times, added a podcast empire that they then had to write down, forced an audiobook bundle on users

who didn't want it, and shipped an AI DJ that nobody asked for. They have not shipped lossless. They have not shipped spatial audio. They have not shipped Apple Music Sing, but they did add an AI DJ. So here's the brutal competitive reality.
Spotify is being squeezed from above and below. From above, Apple Music has the resources of Apple behind it.

cmds.text: Spotify is being squeezed from abov…
Apple Music has the resources of Ap…

scenes.phrase: Apple can outspend Spotify on licensing, on features, on marketing. Apple can afford to ship lossless at no extra cost because Apple can absorb the bandwidth hit. Spotify can't.
From below, YouTube Music is bundled with YouTube Premium, which means millions of subscribers get YouTube Music essentially for free as part of a broader subscription. Amazon Music is

cmds.text: YouTube Music is bundled with YouTu…

scenes.phrase: bundled with Prime. Spotify is the only major music service that has to stand on its own. This is why Spotify is raising prices. They have no other lever. They can't subsidize from a

cmds.text: bundled with Prime. Spotify is the only major musi…

scenes.phrase: hardware business. They can't bundle with a broader subscription that has nothing to do with music. They have to extract more from their existing subscribers because their existing subscribers are their only revenue source. And the market is noticing.

Spotify's market share in the US has been slowly eroding. Not dramatically— they're still the largest music streaming service globally— but the trend is concerning for them. Apple Music has grown to around 30% market share in the US.

scenes.phrase: to all of this has been: more price hikes, more pivots, more AI features. They're trying to convince Wall Street that they have a path to continued growth, while their actual core product—
the music streaming app that you pay for— is, by every measurable metric, getting worse. There's a question nobody at Spotify wants to answer: if your competitors can pay artists more,

cmds.text: the music streaming app that you pay for— is, by e…

scenes.phrase: ship better features, and charge less or the same, while you raise prices every year, ship fewer features, and pay artists less, what exactly is your value proposition? What are you offering that
they don't? The honest answer is playlists. Spotify's playlist curation, even after all the criticism I just gave it, is still better than Apple Music's. And Discover Weekly, even watered down,

scenes.phrase: users, the answer is yes. For now. Spotify is sticky. Your playlists are there. Your friends are there. Your Wrapped is there.

Switching is annoying. But stickiness has limits. And every price hike tests those limits. Let's talk about the man running this whole show. Because his story is the most revealing part of all of this. Daniel Ek, the founder and CEO of

deciding that what he really wants to do with his wealth is build the next generation of weapons systems, while the music platform that made him rich is, by every measure, decaying.

scenes.phrase: that his time and money are better spent building AI for fighter jets than fixing the company he founded. Spotify artists are paid fractions of a cent. Spotify hasn't shipped a promised lossless feature in 5 years. Spotify's podcast

And the CEO's attention is elsewhere. Ek still shows up to earnings calls. He still gives interviews about Spotify's future.

scenes.phrase: Why? Because Spotify finally turned profitable. After years of operating at a loss, after years of burning cash to acquire users, build the podcast empire,
expand into new markets, Spotify started making real money in 2024. Their operating margin went positive.

cmds.text: Spotify started making real money i…

scenes.phrase: Their free cash flow exploded. They reported record profits quarter after quarter. The stock took off. How did they turn profitable?

scenes.phrase: Not by growing subscribers. Subscriber growth has slowed. Not by launching successful new products. The podcast empire collapsed. Audiobooks are a niche. AI features are fluff.
They turned profitable by raising prices and cutting costs. The prices, we've covered. 4 hikes in 3 years.
50% more revenue per subscriber. The cost cuts are less visible but more brutal. Spotify has laid off thousands of employees since 2023. They shut down entire divisions.
They killed shows. They restructured deals. They squeezed every dollar out of their existing operations. And here's the most important cost cut of all: they didn't raise artist royalties.
Per-stream payouts went down. The 1,000-stream demonetization rule redirected money from small artists to major labels.
Discovery Mode let them pay lower rates to artists who opted in. Every lever Spotify had to reduce the percentage of revenue that went to artists, they pulled. The result?

cmds.text: Discovery Mode let them pay lower rates to artists…

scenes.phrase: Spotify's gross margin improved from around 26% in 2022 to over 30% in 2025.

That 4 percentage point improvement on a $15 billion revenue base is $600 million in additional profit, per year, taken directly out of the royalty pool that should have gone to artists. So when you read that Spotify's stock
is at an all-time high, that they're more profitable than ever, that Wall Street loves them— understand what that means. It means they've figured out how to extract more money from

cmds.text: is at an all-time high, that they're mor…

scenes.phrase: you and from artists while delivering less. That's what their profitability is built on. Price hikes. Cost cuts. Lower royalties.
Higher margins. Wall Street is celebrating. Artists are suffering. Listeners are paying more. The CEO is selling shares to build weapons. The product is decaying.

scenes.phrase: This is what a successful tech company looks like in 2026. And the most maddening part? Spotify knows.

scenes.phrase: They know the artists are unhappy. They know the listeners are frustrated. They know the listeners are frustrated. They know the competition is catching up. They just don't care, because the stock price is the only metric that matters to them, and the stock price is going up.

scenes.phrase: But there's one more thing they do that I haven't covered yet. The thing that makes everyone forgive them every December.
Wrapped. Let's talk about Wrapped. Every December, Spotify releases Wrapped. Your year in music. Your top artists, top songs, top genres, total minutes listened.
A slick slideshow with bright colors and shareable graphics. Instagram fills up with Wrapped screenshots. Twitter fills up with Wrapped screenshots. TikTok fills up with Wrapped reactions.

cmds.text: A slick slideshow with bright colors and…

scenes.phrase: Wrapped is brilliant. It's the most successful marketing campaign in music history. It turns every Spotify user into a free billboard for Spotify. Every December, without fail.

cmds.text: It turns every Spotify user into a …

scenes.phrase: But here's what Wrapped actually is. It's a distraction. While you're sharing your top 5 artists, Spotify is raising your subscription price. While you're bragging about your 100,000

cmds.text: But here's what Wrapped actually is. It's a distra…

scenes.phrase: minutes listened, Spotify is cutting per-stream royalties to artists. While you're celebrating your music taste, Spotify is shipping AI-generated slop into your playlists.
Wrapped is the dessert they serve you so you don't notice that the main course got smaller and more expensive. And Wrapped has gotten worse too. The 2024 Wrapped was widely criticized

for being less detailed, less interesting, less shareable than previous years. Spotify added AI-generated podcast commentary about your listening habits, which most users found annoying. They removed some of the granular stats
that power users loved. They turned a beloved annual tradition into another vehicle for their AI features. Wrapped is also, structurally, free user research for Spotify.

Every time you share your Wrapped, you're telling Spotify, and the world, what kind of music you like, how much you listen, what genres you gravitate toward, how loyal you are to certain artists. Spotify uses this data to train their recommendation

cmds.text: Every time you share your Wrapped, you'r…

scenes.phrase: algorithms, to sell targeted advertising on the free tier, to negotiate with major labels for better terms. You're giving them all of that, for free, every December and thanking them for the privilege. But here's the thing

about Wrapped. It works. People love it. They look forward to it. They share it. They defend Spotify because of it. Wrapped is the single best argument that Spotify still makes something people genuinely

cmds.text: about Wrapped. It works. People love it.…

scenes.phrase: love, which makes it hard to argue that Spotify is a bad company. Because if they were, why would you look forward to their annual recap so much? The answer is that Spotify isn't a bad company. They're a good company that's been slowly,

cmds.text: love, which makes it hard to argue that Spotify is…

scenes.phrase: deliberately optimizing for the wrong things. They optimized for engagement instead of discovery. For margin instead of artist welfare. For Wall Street instead of musicians. They didn't

cmds.text: deliberately optimizing for the wrong th…

scenes.phrase: become evil. They became efficient. And efficiency, when your product is art, is corrosive. So what should you do? Let's talk about that. So, You've listened to all of this. You've heard the numbers.
You've heard about the price hikes, the royalty cuts, the AI slop, the podcast collapse, the audiobook bundling, the broken hi-fi promise, the algorithmic decay, the CEO's exit, the stock
bubble. And you're wondering, should I cancel Spotify? Maybe. Here's my honest take. Spotify is still, in many ways, the best music streaming app. The recommendation engine, even watered down,

cmds.text: bubble. And you're wondering, should I cancel Spot…

scenes.phrase: is the best in the business. The playlist ecosystem is unmatched. The social features— collaborative playlists, Blend, Wrapped— are genuinely fun. The catalog is huge. The app works well. If you care about music discovery, Spotify is
still the easiest place to find new music. But every year, the gap between Spotify and its competitors narrows.

cmds.text: still the easiest place to find new…
the gap between Spotify and its com…

scenes.phrase: Apple Music has lossless. Apple Music has spatial audio. Apple Music pays artists twice as much. YouTube Music is bundled with YouTube Premium. Tidal pays artists even more than Apple. Even Amazon Music
has lossless now. If you care about audio quality, switch to Apple Music or Tidal. If you care about artist compensation, switch to Apple Music or Tidal. If you want lossless,
switch to literally anyone else. If you're tired of price hikes, switch to YouTube Music, which is bundled with YouTube Premium and often effectively cheaper. If you want

cmds.text: which is bundled with YouTube Premi…

scenes.phrase: the best playlist curation and the best recommendation engine, and you're willing to pay for it, stay with Spotify. Just know what you're paying for, and who you're paying, and what they're doing with the money.

cmds.text: the best playlist curation and the best recommenda…

scenes.phrase: Here's what I'd actually recommend. Don't make a moral decision. Make a practical one. Try Apple Music for a month.

cmds.text: Here's what I'd actually recommend. Don't make a m…

scenes.phrase: Try Tidal. See if you miss Spotify. Most people don't miss it as much as they think they will. Your playlists can be transferred with tools like Soundeze, and tune my music. Your friends will

still be on Spotify if you want to make collaborative playlists. You can keep Spotify free tier for social stuff and pay for Apple Music for actual listening. And— this is the most important part— buy music. Buy albums on Bandcamp.

Buy vinyl. Buy merch. Go to concerts. Buy directly from artists whenever you can. Streaming was never going to sustain a music ecosystem. It was always going to extract from it. The only way to actually support artists is to pay them directly,

cmds.text: Buy vinyl. Buy merch. Go to concerts. Buy directly…

scenes.phrase: in transactions that aren't mediated by a streaming platform's royalty math. Spotify has a serious problem.
Their product is decaying. Their artists are starving. Their CEO has checked out. Their stock is a bubble.

cmds.text: Their product is decaying. Their artists…

scenes.phrase: Their pivots have failed. Their promises have been broken. Their competition is catching up. But Spotify's biggest problem isn't any of those things. Spotify's biggest problem is that they've forgotten what they're for. They were supposed to be the

cmds.text: Their pivots have failed. Their promises have been…

scenes.phrase: platform that gave every artist a fair shot and every listener access to every song ever recorded. They were supposed to be the future of music. Instead, they've become a cautionary
tale. A story about what happens when a company that promised to democratize an industry decides to squeeze it instead.

cmds.text: tale. A story about what happens when a company th…

scenes.phrase: You've paid Spotify more money every year since 2023. The artists you love have been paid less per stream every year since 2023. And Spotify's stock has gone up every year since 2023.
3 numbers. 3 trajectories. All connected. You can decide for yourself what they add up to, but if you ask me, they add up to a serious problem.
